event: Moses saved from the water by Pharaoh's daughter
symbols: infant drawn from the water — divine providence preserving the future deliverer of Israel
Pharaoh's daughter rescuing the child — God using even the enemy's household to fulfill his purposes
river among lush vegetation — the Nile that was meant to destroy Hebrew infants becomes the means of salvation
Egyptian palace in background — the seat of oppression that unwittingly nurtures Israel's liberator
description: A lush, classical landscape with tall trees framing a scene by a riverbank. In the foreground right, a group of women in colorful robes gather around a central figure (Pharaoh's daughter) who bends down or reaches toward an infant (Moses) being lifted from the water or reeds. Other attendants assist or watch with animated gestures. The setting is a verdant garden-like landscape with luxuriant green foliage, trees, and flowering plants. In the background, Egyptian-style architecture — arched gateways and a palace or tower — is visible under a soft blue sky with clouds.
theological_description: Taunay depicts the pivotal rescue of the infant Moses from the Nile, as recorded in Exodus 2:1-10. Pharaoh had decreed that every Hebrew boy born should be thrown into the Nile (Exodus 1:22). Moses's mother, unable to hide him any longer, placed him in a waterproofed basket among the reeds. Pharaoh's own daughter came to bathe, discovered the basket, and 'saw the baby. He was crying, and she felt sorry for him. "This is one of the Hebrew babies," she said' (Exodus 2:6). In a supreme irony of divine providence, the child sentenced to death by Pharaoh's decree was rescued by Pharaoh's daughter and raised in the royal palace. Moses's sister Miriam, watching from a distance (Exodus 2:4), arranged for their own mother to nurse him. This rescue is an act of God's sovereign protection of the one who would eventually lead Israel out of Egypt. The name Moses itself means 'drawn out' (Exodus 2:10) — he who was drawn from the water would draw an entire nation from bondage. Stephen, in his speech before the Sanhedrin, highlighted this providence: 'At that time Moses was born, and he was no ordinary child. For three months he was cared for by his family. When he was placed outside, Pharaoh's daughter took him and brought him up as her own son' (Acts 7:20-21). Hebrews 11:23 credits the faith of Moses's parents: 'By faith Moses's parents hid him for three months, because they saw he was no ordinary child.'

scripture_refs.reason: The full narrative of Moses placed in a basket, found by Pharaoh's daughter, and adopted
Pharaoh's decree to throw every Hebrew boy into the Nile — the threat Moses escaped
Stephen's retelling of Moses's rescue and upbringing in Pharaoh's household
By faith Moses's parents hid him — the faith that preceded this rescue
